title: Anomaly detection

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Anomaly detection; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: scikit-learn User Guide; 1.9.x target, accessed 2026-07; User Guide sections 2, 3.4.7, and 7.5: clustering, novelty/outlier detection, decomposition, and evaluation; atomic topic: Anomaly detection
sources.claim: Anomaly detection scores observations as unusual relative to a defined reference population and representation; thresholds convert scores into alerts whose value depends on prevalence, review cost, and consequences.

sources.locator: NIST AI Risk Management Framework; AI RMF 1.0; revision status monitored; AI RMF Map/Measure outcomes for context, harmful bias, feedback, monitoring, and affected stakeholders; atomic topic: Anomaly detection
sources.claim: Anomaly detection scores observations as unusual relative to a defined reference population and representation; thresholds convert scores into alerts whose value depends on prevalence, review cost, and consequences.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Anomaly detection scores observations as unusual relative to a defined reference population and representation; thresholds convert scores into alerts whose value depends on prevalence, review cost, and consequences.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A robust univariate baseline flags large median absolute deviations, then reports alert rate and reviewer-confirmed precision instead of calling every outlier fraud. Your goal is to design and evaluate a simple anomaly detector without assuming every outlier is harmful. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Data and experimental workflow; Bias, variance and regularization; Clustering; Classification and probabilities. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Anomaly detection scores observations as unusual relative to a defined reference population and representation; thresholds convert scores into alerts whose value depends on prevalence, review cost, and consequences. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Rare is not harmful, harmful can be common, contaminated training shifts the reference, tail estimates are unstable, labels are scarce, and feedback from reviewed alerts changes future data. The learner must reject this overclaim: An outlier score is a probability of fraud or danger, and a stricter threshold always improves the detector.

includes: Define unusual relative to a reference population and evaluate rare-event alerts with review costs.
Design and evaluate a simple anomaly detector without assuming every outlier is harmful.
excludes: Rare is not harmful, harmful can be common, contaminated training shifts the reference, tail estimates are unstable, labels are scarce, and feedback from reviewed alerts changes future data.

outcomes: Design and evaluate a simple anomaly detector without assuming every outlier is harmful.

examples.title: Anomaly detection: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A robust univariate baseline flags large median absolute deviations, then reports alert rate and reviewer-confirmed precision instead of calling every outlier fraud.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A robust univariate baseline flags large median absolute deviations, then reports alert rate and reviewer-confirmed precision instead of calling every outlier fraud.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Define unusual relative to a reference and evaluate thresholded alerts with prevalence, review capacity, false alarms, misses, drift, and domain confirmation.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Define unusual relative to a reference and evaluate thresholded alerts with prevalence, review capacity, false alarms, misses, drift, and domain confirmation.

examples.title: Anomaly detection: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A new seasonal pattern triggers many alerts, so monitoring distinguishes distribution drift from individual harmful events.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A new seasonal pattern triggers many alerts, so monitoring distinguishes distribution drift from individual harmful events.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Rare is not harmful, harmful can be common, contaminated training shifts the reference, tail estimates are unstable, labels are scarce, and feedback from reviewed alerts changes future data.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: An outlier score is a probability of fraud or danger, and a stricter threshold always improves the detector.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and evaluate a simple anomaly detector without assuming every outlier is harmful.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: An outlier score is a probability of fraud or danger, and a stricter threshold always improves the detector.
misconceptions.correction: Define unusual relative to a reference and evaluate thresholded alerts with prevalence, review capacity, false alarms, misses, drift, and domain confirmation.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: An outlier score is a probability of fraud or danger, and a stricter threshold always improves the detector.

analogy.example: an exploratory sorting table whose representation, distance, projection, reference population, and exposure policy determine visible patterns can introduce Anomaly detection only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly scoring, and recommendation
limitations: algorithmic groups and outliers are parameter-dependent summaries rather than discovered human categories or harms

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and evaluate a simple anomaly detector without assuming every outlier is harmful. Keep this boundary in view: Rare is not harmful, harmful can be common, contaminated training shifts the reference, tail estimates are unstable, labels are scarce, and feedback from reviewed alerts changes future data.
